title: 5 Things about Twelve Years, a condensed hex and dungeon crawl (solo RPG Friday)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yrcwoFLtS5Y

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes a quick look at Twelve Years, a solo RPG zine by Max Moon, timed to the imminent Kickstarter for its expansion. You roll up a party of four archetypes — hermit, rogue, fey folk, giant — equip them (loot can be mundane, magical, or trapped enough to kill a hero before play begins), and then have twelve in-game years to find a magic crown, place it on the Lich King's head, and destroy him. The game splits cleanly into a D6-driven hex crawl across varied terrain and a graph-paper dungeon crawl, with the whole party levelling as a single unit.

He frames the review as five things. One: it delivers a genuinely condensed crawl in a tiny footprint with minimal effort. Two: the world feels unique and inviting without burying you in lore. Three: the presentation is lovely, bar the microscopic quick-play sheet. Four: the win condition is muddled — sources disagree on whether the crown must be magical, and a hinted second path goes undefined. Five: he wants more variety; one campaign turned up roughly seven "lost nobles," exposing how dice-dependent the experience is. He's hopeful the expansion adds variety and a clearer objective.

----

Do you enjoy games that live and die by the dice, embracing the cold streaks for the stories they tell — or does that randomness frustrate you?
